summary: In these 1977 and 1978 shareholder letters, Warren Buffett outlines his core philosophies on capital allocation, measuring managerial performance, and equity investing. He emphasizes the importance of return on equity over earnings per share, the necessity of operating in businesses with economic 'tailwinds,' and his strategy of buying small pieces of outstanding businesses at auction-market discounts.

principles.statement: Return on equity capital is a more appropriate measure of managerial economic performance than earnings per share.
principles.rationale: Because businesses customarily add to their equity base year to year through retained earnings, a simple increase in earnings per share is not noteworthy. Even a dormant savings account produces rising earnings due to compounding.
principles.source_quote: Except for special cases... we believe a more appropriate measure of managerial economic performance to be return on equity capital.

principles.statement: It is crucial to operate in businesses where tailwinds prevail rather than headwinds.
principles.rationale: In a difficult, capital-intensive business with commodity products (like textiles), even excellent management will likely only achieve modest results. In a good business, you can make mistakes and still achieve satisfactory overall performance.
principles.source_quote: One of the lessons your management has learned - and, unfortunately, sometimes re-learned - is the importance of being in businesses where tailwinds prevail rather than headwinds.

principles.statement: Marketable equity securities should be selected in the exact same way one would evaluate a business for acquisition in its entirety.
principles.rationale: Buying a stock is buying a small piece of a business. It is unsound to become mesmerized by short-term stock price behavior or near-term earnings trends when purchasing equities.
principles.source_quote: We select our marketable equity securities in much the same way we would evaluate a business for acquisition in its entirety.

principles.statement: Retained earnings of non-controlled companies are just as valuable as dividends if the company can employ the capital advantageously.
principles.rationale: If a company has excellent prospects for profitable employment of capital, retaining earnings will eventually translate into market value greater than 100 cents on the dollar for owners.
principles.source_quote: We are not at all unhappy when our wholly-owned businesses retain all of their earnings if they can utilize internally those funds at attractive rates. Why should we feel differently about retention of earnings by companies in which we hold small equity interests...?

principles.statement: It is often better to accept passive participation in excellent management than to control a mediocre operation.
principles.rationale: If an outstanding business is run better than you could run it yourself, direct ownership and control offer no important advantages and may actually yield a worse management result.
principles.source_quote: In effect, we can obtain a better management result through non-control than control. This is an unorthodox view, but one we believe to be sound.

frameworks.name: Marketable Equity Selection
frameworks.when_to_apply: When evaluating common stocks for investment.
steps: Ensure the business is one that you can understand.
Verify that the business has favorable long-term prospects.
Confirm the business is operated by honest and competent people.
Ensure the business is available at a very attractive price.
frameworks.source_quote: We want the business to be (1) one that we can understand, (2) with favorable long-term prospects, (3) operated by honest and competent people, and (4) available at a very attractive price.

mental_models.name: Tailwinds vs. Headwinds
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the underlying economic characteristics of an industry. A business with 'tailwinds' (like certain insurance niches) can succeed despite mistakes, while a business with 'headwinds' (like textiles) will struggle to produce adequate returns on capital despite energetic and excellent management.
mental_models.example: Buffett contrasts the highly profitable but mistake-prone insurance expansion with the textile business, where 'even very good management probably can average only modest results.'

mental_models.name: Auction Pricing Mechanism vs. Negotiated Sales
mental_models.description: Viewing the stock market as an auction that occasionally offers pro-rata portions of outstanding businesses at huge discounts compared to the valuations those same businesses would command in negotiated corporate acquisitions.
mental_models.example: Buffett notes that buying SAFECO stock in the market allowed Berkshire to pay 'less than 100 cents on the dollar for the best company in the business, when far more than 100 cents on the dollar is being paid for mediocre companies in corporate transactions.'

heuristics: Concentrate holdings when convinced of attractiveness; avoid buying a little of this or that when only lukewarm.
Welcome lower market prices of stocks you own as an opportunity to acquire more of a good thing at a better price.
The manager of a tightly-run operation usually continues to find additional methods to curtail costs, while the manager of a high-cost operation finds new ways to add to overhead.
It is easier to buy a good insurance business than to create one.

signature_quotes.text: After all, even a totally dormant savings account will produce steadily rising interest earnings each year because of compounding.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why 'record' earnings per share is a flawed metric for managerial performance.

signature_quotes.text: One of the lessons your management has learned - and, unfortunately, sometimes re-learned - is the importance of being in businesses where tailwinds prevail rather than headwinds.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the difference between the forgiving economics of the insurance business and the punishing economics of the textile business.

signature_quotes.text: Just as it would be foolish to focus unduly on short-term prospects when acquiring an entire company, we think it equally unsound to become mesmerized by prospective near term earnings or recent trends in earnings when purchasing small pieces of a company; i.e., marketable common stocks.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his long-term approach to holding equity investments.

signature_quotes.text: We are not concerned with whether the market quickly revalues upward securities that we believe are selling at bargain prices. In fact, we prefer just the opposite...
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that as a net buyer of securities, lower prices are advantageous.

anti_patterns.description: Defining 'record' earnings simply as a new high in earnings per share.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores the fact that businesses customarily add to their equity base each year. A 5% increase in EPS on a 10% increase in equity capital is not an impressive managerial performance.
It leads to inadequate industry rate levels. If major competitors don't know their true costs and underprice their policies, the competitive 'fall-out' hits everyone in the industry.

anti_patterns.description: Chasing volume at foolish prices in the insurance business.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: When markets loosen and rates become inadequate, writing policies at a loss destroys value. It requires unusual managerial discipline to let competitors take away business rather than match foolish prices.

anti_patterns.description: Evaluating a business based on a single-year snapshot.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Short-term results can obscure long-term economic realities. A business might look like an economic powerhouse in one year but suffer massive aggregate losses over the following decade.
